file_name: Mihaela et al. - 2023 - A Convolutional Neural Network for skin lesion classification in Dermoscopic Images using discrete w.json

did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) implemented in Python with Keras/TensorFlow to perform binary classification of skin lesions (melanoma vs. nevi) from dermoscopic images in the MED-NODE dataset. The method combines discrete wavelet transforms (Haar and Daubechies) for feature extraction with CNNs for classification.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work involving the development and testing of a Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) combined with discrete wavelet transforms for skin lesion classification. It details specific methods (Haar and Daubechies wavelets, CNN architecture), datasets (MED-NODE), and reports new results (accuracy, precision, recall, F1-score) from experiments conducted on 170 images. Although published in a conference proceedings (2023 European Control Conference), it contains original research findings rather than summarizing prior literature.

title: A Convolutional Neural Network for skin lesion classification in Dermoscopic Images using discrete wavelet transform
brief_summary: This study investigates a binary classification framework for distinguishing melanoma from nevi in dermoscopic images using Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) combined with discrete wavelet transforms. The authors utilized the MED-NODE dataset, comprising 170 images, and applied Haar and Daubechies wavelets to extract Low-Low (LL) and High-High (HH) subbands for feature extraction. Five distinct CNN architectures were tested, each fed with either raw images or specific wavelet-derived subimages. The model achieved its highest performance when using Haar wavelet LL subimages, resulting in 85% accuracy, 85% precision, 85% recall, and an 85% F1-score on the test set. The results indicate that low-frequency subbands significantly improve classification metrics compared to high-frequency bands or raw images.

lesion_types_studied.details: One of the two binary classes in the MED-NODE dataset (70 cases) used for classification. Mapped to label 0 in the CNN.
One of the two binary classes in the MED-NODE dataset (100 cases) used for classification. Mapped to label 1 in the CNN.

all_task_types.target: Binary classification of skin lesions into melanoma (label 0) and nevi/naevus (label 1) categories.
all_task_types.details: The paper explicitly states the objective is 'skin lesion classification' and 'binary classify the images in two categories: melanoma and naevus'. The model outputs discrete diagnostic labels (0 or 1) using a sigmoid activation function and binary crossentropy loss. Performance is evaluated using accuracy, precision, recall, and F1-score on a test set from the MED-NODE dataset.

datasets_used.name: MED-NODE
datasets_used.description: A public dataset belonging to the Department of Dermatology of the University Medical Center Groningen, containing dermoscopic images of skin lesions.

use.training: Used for training the CNN models (70% of the dataset).
use.validation: Used for validation and hyperparameter tuning (15% of the dataset).
use.testing: Used for final evaluation of the model performance (15% of the dataset).

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance was evaluated using accuracy, precision, recall, and F1-score derived from confusion matrices on a test set.

MED-NODE.notes: The best performance was achieved using Haar2_LL and db2_LL subimages with 85% accuracy, precision, recall, and F1-score on the test set. The worst performance was observed with db2_HH subimages (50% accuracy). The paper notes that low-frequency subbands (LL) significantly improve classification metrics compared to high-frequency bands (HH) or raw images.

architecture_details.framework: Python 3.7.15 with Keras library from TensorFlow framework

layers.details: Applied just before the output layer

architecture_details.input_preprocessing: Data standardized to [0, 1] range via normalization layer; image augmentation applied (rotation 10 degrees, horizontal flip)

preprocessing_techniques: 2D discrete wavelet transform (Haar and Daubechies) to generate Low-Low (LL) and High-High (HH) subimages
Data standardization to [0, 1] range via normalization layer
Image augmentation: rotation (10 degrees) and horizontal flip

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Random rotation with a degree range of 10 degrees.

conclusions: The proposed framework combining discrete wavelet transforms (Haar and Daubechies) with Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) is effective for binary classification of melanoma and nevi in dermoscopic images.
Low-Low (LL) subimages generated from 2D wavelet decomposition significantly improve classification performance compared to High-High (HH) subimages or raw original images.
The best performance was achieved using Haar2_LL and db2_LL subimages, resulting in 85% accuracy, precision, recall, and F1-score on the test set.
The worst performance was observed with db2_HH subimages, which yielded only 50% accuracy, indicating that high-frequency bands are less suitable for this classification task.
The study acknowledges that small datasets can lead to overfitting, which was mitigated in this work through data augmentation (rotation and horizontal flip) and dropout layers.
Future work aims to utilize the Keras Tuner library for hyperparameter optimization and to feed CNNs with all subimage types (LL, HH, LH, HL) to potentially further boost model performance.
important_remarks: The study relies on a very small dataset (170 images total: 70 melanoma, 100 nevi), which limits statistical power and generalizability.
The dataset (MED-NODE) is imbalanced, with nearly twice as many nevi (100) as melanoma cases (70).
The paper acknowledges that small datasets can lead to overfitting, where the model learns unwanted details like noise.
No external validation was performed; the model was only tested on the same public MED-NODE dataset used for training and validation.
Hyperparameter tuning was not performed using systematic optimization methods (like Grid Search or Keras Tuner); instead, values were documented from similar studies and the Keras official library.
The best-performing model achieved 100% training accuracy and 98% validation accuracy, indicating potential overfitting despite the use of dropout (0.3) and data augmentation.
The test set is extremely small (40 images), making performance metrics (85% accuracy) less robust and potentially unstable.
The paper does not report confidence intervals or p-values for the reported metrics.
Data leakage risk exists if the augmentation techniques (rotation, flip) were applied before the train/validation/test split, although the text implies standard pipeline configuration.
The study only evaluates Low-Low (LL) and High-High (HH) subbands, ignoring Low-High (LH) and High-Low (HL) bands, which the authors note as future work.
